caseTitle: Janet Brooks — The drug fever that almost killed her
_status_notes: 3 timepoints (TP1 admission + variceal bleed/TIPS + post-TIPS fevers; TP2 negative infectious workup, worsening, family raises CMO; TP3 post-CMO unexpected recovery → presumed drug fever). 5 questions. Weights 0.6/0.8/1.0/0.9/0.5 = 3.8 total. Source: Management Case Bank, Case 5 (original discussants Wendy Stead, Emily Rubin; finalized by Jason Hom).
description: A 3-stage ICU case following Janet Brooks, a 55-year-old woman with alcohol-related cirrhosis who presents with involuntary weight loss, suffers a variceal hemorrhage requiring emergent TIPS, and then develops persistent fevers and refractory shock. An exhaustive infectious workup is negative, yet the team — anchored on occult infection — keeps escalating antibiotics as she deteriorates toward a comfort-measures decision. After antibiotics are stopped she unexpectedly recovers: the cause was a drug fever and drug-associated neurotoxicity that was never fully considered. The case tests cognitive humility and anti-anchoring: keeping a broad (non-infectious) differential, being willing to stop a presumed-necessary therapy, and avoiding irreversible decisions while a reversible, unconsidered diagnosis remains.
warning: Plan to dedicate about 26 minutes uninterrupted. Pausing is permitted but stops the timer.
instructions: You are caring for a real patient across three timepoints. Review the chart, then respond to each clinical question. Your responses are graded against a rubric; the case's intended teaching points are revealed at the end.
